source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 7.3.3-7.3.4 usermod/userdel (pp. 108-109)

questions.q: Change the UID of the user carine to 1044.
questions.why: usermod -u sets a new UID; afterwards the user's files still on the old UID must be reassigned (e.g. find / -uid OLD -exec chown NEW: {} \;).

questions.q: Rename the login 'carine' to 'karine' (change the login name only).
questions.why: usermod -l changes the login name; you should also rename the home directory to match.

questions.q: Lock the account 'test1' (add a ! at the start of its shadow password field).
questions.why: usermod -L locks the account; -U unlocks it again.

questions.q: What is the critical difference between 'usermod -G' and 'usermod -aG' for supplementary groups?
options: -G REPLACES (overwrites) the user's supplementary groups; -aG APPENDS to them
-G is for primary groups; -aG is for supplementary groups
questions.why: -G overwrites the whole supplementary-group list (dropping the others); -a with -G appends without removing existing memberships. Forgetting -a is a classic way to accidentally remove a user from groups.

questions.q: Append the user test1 to the supplementary group groupD WITHOUT removing their other groups.
questions.why: -aG appends the group; using -G alone here would have replaced all of test1's supplementary groups with just groupD.

questions.q: Delete the user carine along with their home directory and mail files.
questions.why: userdel -r removes the account and its home directory plus /var/spool/mail files; it also deletes the user's same-named primary group.

questions.q: What condition must hold before a user can be modified or deleted?
options: The user must be logged out and have no running processes
The user must be logged in
The system must be in single-user mode
The user must first be added to the wheel group
questions.why: To be modified or deleted, a user must be disconnected with no running processes.
